caveats: a: No published source supplies a six-digit, wage-weighted AI-exposure measure for this heterogeneous code.
a: The broader 4881 occupation mix includes aircraft maintenance and air traffic control, which the lens excludes.
a: The frozen compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a cross-code harmonization adjustment.
rho: Published adoption figures are airport-level and not specific to LMM outsourced operators.
rho: A reported delay reduction is not the same as a labor reduction.
rho: Cybersecurity, system interoperability, labor agreements, and airport permits may delay use.
e: NAICS classification is unusually heterogeneous and establishment self-classification may differ by site.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged at ESTIMATE quality and may misplace asset-heavy operators around the EBITDA band.
e: Eligibility may vary materially between commercial-airport handlers and general-aviation FBOs.
s5: The Census acquisition statistic spans all employer industries and is not a longitudinal transfer rate.
s5: Owner retirement, family succession, and corporate divestiture have different probabilities of producing an acquirable control stake.
s5: Airport leases and airline contracts may require consent or rebidding after a change of control.
q: Commercial handling, FBO, de-icing, and airport-management contracts have different billing structures.
q: The IATA template is global and common but not mandatory.
q: Volume growth, service penalties, and equipment capital costs are separate from retention of an implemented gross benefit.
d5: National aviation growth may concentrate at hubs outside the target firms' footprints.
d5: Passenger, cargo, general-aviation, and weather-service demand do not grow at the same rate.
d5: The FAA forecast assumes adequate infrastructure and is exposed to macroeconomic, fuel-price, and geopolitical shocks.
o: The GAO worker population includes some airport-service occupations outside the narrowed NAICS lens.
o: Airline self-handling can remove outsourced demand while leaving the underlying service quantity intact.
o: Operator-required does not necessarily mean the same local staffing level if remote coordination and autonomous equipment expand.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Airlines, airport authorities, aircraft operators, and tenants purchase contracted services through negotiated ground-handling agreements, per-turn or activity charges, recurring FBO service relationships, and airport-management contracts. Revenue generally combines volume-linked service fees with contractual operating and quality obligations.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS code combines asset-heavy airport ownership and space rental with outsourced operational services. The lens is narrowed to recurring third-party operating services because airport landlord economics and labor-based handling economics cannot be screened coherently together.
lens.excluded_activities: Public airport authorities, airline-captive ground operations, pure hangar or gate landlords, businesses whose economics are predominantly fuel resale or real estate, air traffic control, aircraft repair, janitorial-only firms, catering, security screening, and non-control investment vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring outsourced airport and fixed-base operational services, including ramp and turnaround coordination, baggage handling, ground direction, aircraft service support, de-icing, runway clearing, and contract airport administration.

a.bridge: BLS reports the occupation mix for the broader support-activities-for-air-transportation industry, and O*NET describes baggage work rather than a wage-weighted AI task share for NAICS 488119. I map planning, dispatch, communications, passenger information, recordkeeping, billing, object recognition, inspection support, and resource allocation to AI exposure, while treating lifting, driving, de-icing, runway work, physical servicing, and safety response as largely unexposed. This can overstate exposure because the BLS population includes aircraft repair and air traffic control, and understate it where computer vision and autonomy reach ramp equipment.
caveats: No published source supplies a six-digit, wage-weighted AI-exposure measure for this heterogeneous code.
The broader 4881 occupation mix includes aircraft maintenance and air traffic control, which the lens excludes.
The frozen compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a cross-code harmonization adjustment.
a.change_evidence: Six-digit OEWS microdata or employer payroll by occupation, paired with task-level time studies at representative FBO, ground-handler, de-icing, and airport-management firms and measured labor changes after AI turnaround deployment.
a.rationale: The broader industry has large frontline occupations such as hand material movers and aircraft service attendants alongside clerical, customer-service, supervisory, and operations roles. AI-integrated turnaround systems can observe events and coordinate parties, but core baggage, ramp, aircraft, and runway tasks remain physical. A roughly one-third wage-weighted opportunity reflects sizable information work without treating physical automation as generative-AI substitution.

d5.bridge: FAA forecasts system passenger and aircraft activity, not a constant-quality quantity index for the narrowed outsourced airport-operations basket. I bracket demand between the 2026-to-2031 growth in controlled-airport operations (about 6%) and system enplanements (about 13%), using 9% as a blended central proxy because handlers and airport services respond to both turns and passengers. The bridge may overstate LMM growth if traffic concentrates at large hubs served by national firms and understate it where business-aviation activity expands faster.
caveats: National aviation growth may concentrate at hubs outside the target firms' footprints.
Passenger, cargo, general-aviation, and weather-service demand do not grow at the same rate.
The FAA forecast assumes adequate infrastructure and is exposed to macroeconomic, fuel-price, and geopolitical shocks.
d5.change_evidence: FAA Terminal Area Forecasts and BTS departures, passengers, and cargo by airport mapped to each eligible firm's customer locations, plus contract-level service units and customer insourcing plans through 2031.
d5.rationale: Commercial passenger volumes and aircraft turns should remain the primary service drivers. FAA's 2026 forecast calls for continued air-travel growth, 1% annual growth in FAA and contract tower operations over the long horizon, and faster commercial than noncommercial activity. A downside below current quantity allows recession or customer insourcing; the high case reflects continued passenger and turbine-business-aviation strength.

caveats: NAICS classification is unusually heterogeneous and establishment self-classification may differ by site.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged at ESTIMATE quality and may misplace asset-heavy operators around the EBITDA band.
Eligibility may vary materially between commercial-airport handlers and general-aviation FBOs.
e.change_evidence: A legal-entity census of the 264 implied firms using Census Business Register/SUSB records, airport concession and tenant lists, SAM and local procurement records, revenue-by-service interviews, and ultimate-parent screening.
e.rationale: The code explicitly includes both operating airports or flying fields and supporting them through hangar rental, baggage, and cargo handling. The narrower lens excludes public entities, airline-captive units, pure landlords, and businesses dominated by out-of-scope fuel or real estate. Against the frozen n=264, I estimate that about three-fifths are genuine recurring outsourced operating-service firms with control-transferable operations.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 488119 ($5,344,997,608) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 488119 ($16,986,810,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0983 (margins.json[48]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Transportation EBITDA/Sales 9.83%); revenue/employee $146,714 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 488119); class <5: 432 firms, avg revenue/firm $659,174 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $64,797 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 150 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,863,700 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $183,202 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 146 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,646,541 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $358,455 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 208 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,278,067 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,010,334 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 56 firms, avg revenue/firm $50,465,696 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,960,778 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 60 firms, avg revenue/firm $182,103,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,900,725 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 264

o.bridge: GAO and O*NET document current physical, safety, security, and passenger-assistance tasks, while IATA documents assistive automation; none measures the year-five operator-required share. I treat physical aircraft servicing, baggage movement, equipment operation, runway work, exception handling, disability assistance, and accountable safety/security functions as operator-required, while allowing routine information, tracking, scheduling, and some equipment movement to be automated or self-served. The proxy can overstate survival if robotics scales quickly and understate it if traffic complexity creates more on-site exception work.
caveats: The GAO worker population includes some airport-service occupations outside the narrowed NAICS lens.
Airline self-handling can remove outsourced demand while leaving the underlying service quantity intact.
Operator-required does not necessarily mean the same local staffing level if remote coordination and autonomous equipment expand.
o.change_evidence: Deployment and labor data for autonomous tugs, baggage robotics, remote ramp control, self-service passenger systems, and de-icing/runway automation, including safety approvals and operator-to-turn ratios.
o.rationale: Most service quantity still requires an accountable on-site operator: workers move baggage and cargo, operate ramp equipment near aircraft, inspect for hazards, assist disabled travelers, clear runways, and respond to disruptions. AI is improving coordination and observation, but the five-year displacement path is primarily augmentation plus selective self-service rather than elimination of the service firm.

q.bridge: IATA documents negotiated ground-handling scope, charges, duration, service levels, and charge-adjustment rights, but does not measure retained AI benefit. I infer that a handler can retain some within-contract labor and coordination savings, while airline procurement, service-level enforcement, minimum-wage adjustments, volume changes, and renewal competition share gains with customers. This may understate capture for differentiated FBO relationships and overstate it for rebid-heavy commodity handling.
caveats: Commercial handling, FBO, de-icing, and airport-management contracts have different billing structures.
The IATA template is global and common but not mandatory.
Volume growth, service penalties, and equipment capital costs are separate from retention of an implemented gross benefit.
q.change_evidence: Representative Annex B pricing schedules, contract duration and renewal data, customer concentration, gross-margin bridges before and after automation, bid-loss data, and FBO membership or subscription terms.
q.rationale: Pricing is contract-specific rather than automatic cost-plus: SGHA Annex B records negotiated services and charges, and the template permits charge adjustments for flight frequency, minimum wages, or process changes affecting cost. That gives operators some protection from input shocks but also gives sophisticated airline customers visibility and renegotiation leverage. A midrange retention share is appropriate across a five-year discounted period.

rho.bridge: SITA's airport technology survey and IATA's turnaround guidance observe adoption intentions and operational capabilities across global airports, not the five-year implemented share of exposed labor at US LMM service firms. I translate current investment and commercially available coordination functions into moderate implementation, discounted for fragmented data, airport and airline integration, safety rules, capital constraints, and the difference between productivity and headcount. The proxy likely overstates LMM implementation because surveyed airport leaders are larger and better capitalized.
caveats: Published adoption figures are airport-level and not specific to LMM outsourced operators.
A reported delay reduction is not the same as a labor reduction.
Cybersecurity, system interoperability, labor agreements, and airport permits may delay use.
rho.change_evidence: US airport-by-airport deployment data, vendor cohort studies reporting controller or dispatcher hours per aircraft turn, contract approvals for autonomous equipment, and implementation interviews with LMM ground handlers and FBOs.
rho.rationale: AI can already synchronize turnaround communications, recognize objects and vehicles, capture service timestamps, and improve resource decisions. Adoption has started, but multi-party data integration is limited and much exposed work sits in safety-sensitive workflows. Implementation should be meaningful in dispatch, planning, customer communications, hiring, training, and documentation, yet slower where benefit requires autonomous airside equipment or customer-system integration.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 1.3% of respondent employer businesses were acquired in 2022, a cross-industry, survivor-based one-year ownership statistic rather than a control-transfer probability for eligible airport-services firms. Repeating that annual incidence mechanically implies roughly 6% over five years; I raise the base to 10% for the mature, locally licensed LMM service population and allow a wide range. The proxy can understate transfers omitted by respondent/survivor selection and overstate qualifying transfers if internal or family changes were counted as acquisitions.
caveats: The Census acquisition statistic spans all employer industries and is not a longitudinal transfer rate.
Owner retirement, family succession, and corporate divestiture have different probabilities of producing an acquirable control stake.
Airport leases and airline contracts may require consent or rebidding after a change of control.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, or DealStats transactions coded to 488119 with an eligible-firm denominator; owner-age and succession surveys of NBAA/FBO and ground-handling members; and review of airport concession change-of-control records.
s5.rationale: No industry-specific owner-age or deal-rate denominator was found. The market has numerous local and regional operators, but airport permissions, equipment, customer contracts, labor transition, and concession change-of-control terms can slow a sale. The estimate therefore assumes a minority of eligible firms complete a qualifying control transfer within five years.

principal_driver: Growing aviation volumes and acute coordination complexity create repeat demand and practical AI-assisted productivity opportunities.
principal_weakness: A heterogeneous NAICS population and strong airline/airport contracting leverage make eligible-firm counts and retained savings uncertain.

sources.supports: Census defines 488119 as operating airports or public flying fields and supporting airport operations through hangar rental and baggage or cargo handling, establishing the code's heterogeneous boundary.
sources.title: North American Industry Classification System — Sector 48-49 Archive

sources.supports: Census product definitions enumerate airport/FBO services, ramp services, baggage handling, de-icing, runway clearing, ground direction, aircraft parking, administration, and facility access.
sources.title: NAPCS Product List for NAICS 4881: Support Services for Air Transportation

sources.supports: BLS reports 225,880 support-activities-for-air-transportation jobs in May 2022 and identifies large occupations including mechanics, hand material movers, aircraft service attendants, customer service, cargo agents, supervisors, and airfield operations specialists.
sources.title: A look at jobs related to air transportation for National Aviation Week

sources.supports: O*NET describes baggage marking, physical luggage transfer, traveler information, disability assistance, records, billing, security participation, equipment operation, inspection, and decision tasks.
sources.title: O*NET — Baggage Porters and Bellhops

sources.supports: IATA says AI can synchronize turnaround communications, recognize objects and vehicles, capture service timing, and improve decisions; it expects timestamp-standard adoption to reduce ground-handling delays by up to 5% globally.
sources.title: IATA — Ground Ops of the Future

sources.supports: SITA reports 73% of airports investing in AI for prediction and automation, with uses in passenger flow and turnaround, while cross-system coordination remains limited.
sources.title: SITA Air Transport IT Insights 2025 — Airports

sources.supports: Deloitte reports airport labor shortages, AI use in recruiting, service reductions from staffing challenges, and an expectation that alternative labor sources rise from 17% of airport payroll in 2023 to 38% in 2030.
sources.title: Automation in aviation and hospitality

sources.supports: GAO documents physical baggage and cargo handling, equipment operation, hazard detection, disability assistance, safety response, and security vigilance performed by private airport service workers.
sources.title: Aviation Workforce: Contributions and Characteristics of Selected Airport Workers

sources.supports: IATA describes the common industry contract template covering scope, negotiated charges, service standards, duration, modification, and termination, including charge-adjustment rights for frequency, minimum wage, and process changes.
sources.title: IATA Standard Ground Handling Agreement and 2023 Update

sources.supports: FAA forecasts controlled-airport operations rising from 58.635 million in 2026 to 62.152 million in 2031 and system enplanements rising from 998 million to 1.131 billion over the same period.
sources.title: FAA Aerospace Forecast Fiscal Years 2026–2046

sources.supports: Census reports that 1.3% of respondent employer businesses were acquired in 2022, a cross-industry ownership-incidence proxy.
sources.title: Money and Being Your Own Boss Are Top Motivators for Owning a Business

title: Other Airport Operations
